patient_history.chief_complaint: left hip pain and enlarging mass with recurrent falls
known_conditions: high-grade undifferentiated pleomorphic sarcoma of left hip

input.patient_transcript:
Doctor: Hi, I'm your doctor. Can you tell me about your symptoms?
Patient: Yes, I had a bump on my hip and felt a lot of pain after I fell 11 months ago.
Doctor: Hmm, I see. What did the emergency room diagnose you with?
Patient: They said it was a hematoma around my hip.
Doctor: I understand. And did your symptoms worsen over time?
Patient: Yes, I had recurrent falls.
Doctor: Okay. Did you have any radiographic imaging done?
Patient: Yes, I had a CT scan without intravenous contrast done six weeks before I was diagnosed with cancer.
Doctor: And what did the CT scan show?
Patient: It confirmed that I didn't have a displaced fracture, but it indicated a tissue collection that was suspicious for a hematoma.
Doctor: I see. And then, did you have an MRI done?
Patient: Yes, I did. The MRI demonstrated a soft-tissue mass in my left hip that was suspicious for sarcoma.
Doctor: Okay, got it. And then you had a consultation with an orthopedic oncologic specialist, right?
Patient: Yes, I did. And I underwent a radical resection of the sarcoma the next day.
Doctor: Okay. I have your pathology report here. It says that you were diagnosed with a high-grade undifferentiated pleomorphic sarcoma. The margins were negative and the closest evidence of cancer was 2 mm within surgical margins.
Patient: Hmm.
Doctor: And after the surgery, you had a chest CT that showed two tiny lung nodules with the larger nodule measuring 4 mm. This indicates a recommended follow-up of three to six months.
Patient: Okay.
Doctor: Also, there was a small hypodense lesion in your spleen that was inadequately visualized.
Patient: Hmm.
Doctor: The tumor stage was established to be Stage III measuring 13.5 cm at its greatest dimension. And the TNM staging was established to be pT2a, cN0, cM0 without evidence of lymph node involvement or distant metastases.
Patient: Okay.
Doctor: You had two follow-up appointments with the surgeon, who recommended a consult to medical and radiation oncology. And you had consultations at two other institutions including a sarcoma clinic.
Patient: Yes, I did.
Doctor: And they recommended adjuvant chemotherapy for you.
Patient: Yes, they did.
Doctor: Okay, I will schedule a follow-up appointment for you. It's important that you follow the recommendations of the medical and radiation oncologists.
Patient: Yes, I will. Thank you, doctor.
input.chart_notes: 67-year-old woman with a rapidly enlarging left hip mass after a fall 11 months earlier. Initially diagnosed as a hematoma around the proximal femur in the emergency department, but symptoms worsened with recurrent falls. CT without contrast showed a nonspecific left thigh tissue collection; subsequent MRI demonstrated a soft-tissue mass in the subcutaneous tissue of the left hip suspicious for sarcoma. She underwent radical resection of the mass one week after MRI. Pathology revealed a 13.5 cm superficial left hip undifferentiated high-grade pleomorphic sarcoma with 50-60% necrosis and negative margins (closest tumor 2 mm). Staging chest CT one week post-op showed two tiny lung nodules (largest 4 mm) and a small hypodense splenic lesion; staged pT2a cN0 cM0 (Stage III). She later received adjuvant radiation (60 Gy) and was followed by oncology; surveillance imaging demonstrated new pulmonary nodules concerning for metastasis and a large left hip fluid collection (seroma vs chronic hematoma vs abscess). She presents to physical therapy with left shoulder, flank, and hip pain, lymphedema of the left lower extremity and abdomen, and functional limitations in sleep, standing, dressing, and household activities; current pain control uses Percocet 10/325 mg twice daily and acetaminophen 500 mg as needed.

prediction.clinical_summary: This 67-year-old woman developed a rapidly enlarging painful left hip mass after a fall, initially treated as a hematoma but later identified on MRI as a soft-tissue sarcoma. She underwent radical resection, and pathology showed a 13.5 cm high-grade undifferentiated pleomorphic sarcoma of the left hip with 50-60% necrosis and negative margins, staged pT2a cN0 cM0 (Stage III). Postoperative imaging showed two tiny pulmonary nodules and a small hypodense splenic lesion, with later surveillance noting new pulmonary nodules concerning for metastasis and a large left hip fluid collection. She currently has ongoing pain, lymphedema of the left lower extremity and abdomen, and functional limitations, managed with Percocet and acetaminophen.

differential_diagnosis.condition: High-grade undifferentiated pleomorphic sarcoma of the left hip
differential_diagnosis.rationale: Most likely and confirmed diagnosis based on MRI showing a suspicious soft-tissue mass and surgical pathology demonstrating a 13.5 cm high-grade undifferentiated pleomorphic sarcoma with negative margins.

differential_diagnosis.condition: Chronic expanding hematoma / postoperative seroma of the left hip
differential_diagnosis.rationale: Initially the lesion was interpreted as a hematoma after trauma, and later surveillance imaging described a large left hip fluid collection with differential including seroma versus chronic hematoma versus abscess.

Postoperative chest CT showed tiny pulmonary nodules, and subsequent surveillance imaging demonstrated new pulmonary nodules concerning for metastatic spread from the known high-grade soft-tissue sarcoma.

drug_interactions.recommendation: Avoid duplicate acetaminophen exposure exceeding the total recommended daily dose. Reconcile all acetaminophen-containing products and counsel the patient to keep total acetaminophen intake within safe limits.

final_report.subjective: 67-year-old woman with history of high-grade undifferentiated pleomorphic sarcoma of the left hip, hypertension, acromegaly status post pituitary tumor resection, and recurrent vertigo. She reports that after a fall 11 months earlier she developed a painful bump on the left hip that was initially diagnosed as a hematoma, but symptoms worsened and she had recurrent falls. She now reports persistent left hip pain as well as left shoulder and flank pain, lymphedema of the left lower extremity and abdomen, and difficulty with sleep, standing, dressing, and household activities. Current pain regimen is Percocet 10/325 mg twice daily and acetaminophen 500 mg as needed.
final_report.objective: Prior CT of the left hip/thigh without contrast showed no displaced fracture but a nonspecific tissue collection suspicious for hematoma. MRI subsequently demonstrated a soft-tissue mass in the subcutaneous tissue of the left hip suspicious for sarcoma. She underwent radical resection; pathology revealed a 13.5 cm superficial left hip high-grade undifferentiated pleomorphic sarcoma with 50-60% necrosis and negative margins, closest margin 2 mm. Postoperative staging chest CT showed two tiny pulmonary nodules, largest 4 mm, and a small hypodense splenic lesion that was inadequately characterized. Tumor staged as pT2a cN0 cM0, Stage III. She later received adjuvant radiation to 60 Gy. Surveillance imaging demonstrated new pulmonary nodules concerning for metastasis and a large left hip fluid collection described as seroma versus chronic hematoma versus abscess. Current medications include oxycodone/acetaminophen and additional acetaminophen.
final_report.assessment: 1. Stage III high-grade undifferentiated pleomorphic sarcoma of the left hip, status post radical resection with negative margins and adjuvant radiation. 2. New pulmonary nodules concerning for metastatic disease. 3. Persistent postoperative/post-treatment left hip pain with functional limitation. 4. Left lower extremity and abdominal lymphedema. 5. Left hip fluid collection, with differential including seroma, chronic hematoma, or abscess. 6. Medication safety concern due to duplicate acetaminophen exposure from Percocet plus as-needed acetaminophen.
final_report.plan: Continue close follow-up with medical and radiation oncology and adhere to sarcoma surveillance recommendations. Monitor pulmonary nodules with interval chest imaging and oncologic assessment for possible metastatic disease. Evaluate the left hip fluid collection clinically and radiographically as indicated to distinguish seroma, chronic hematoma, or abscess. Continue pain management but reconcile acetaminophen-containing medications and counsel on maximum safe daily acetaminophen dose. Continue physical therapy and lymphedema management for left lower extremity/abdominal swelling, pain, and functional limitations. Maintain follow-up with the surgical/oncology team for recurrence surveillance and symptom management.
